topic: Barrett's oesophagus — surveillance and cancer risk
clinicalStem: A 58-year-old obese man with a 15-year history of heartburn undergoes an OGD for surveillance. Biopsy confirms intestinal metaplasia with low-grade dysplasia at the gastro-oesophageal junction. He takes a daily PPI and has no dysphagia or weight loss. What is the most appropriate next step in his management?
options: Discharge from surveillance as low-grade dysplasia rarely progresses
Repeat OGD in 6 months with quadrantic biopsies every 1 cm through the Barrett's segment
Proceed immediately to oesophagectomy
Increase PPI to twice daily and re-scope in 5 years
Refer for Helicobacter pylori eradication and repeat biopsy in 12 months
explanation: Low-grade dysplasia in Barrett's oesophagus carries a meaningful risk of progression to high-grade dysplasia or adenocarcinoma, so closer endoscopic surveillance every 6 months is recommended to detect progression early. Quadrantic biopsies every 1 cm through the entire segment (Prague protocol) maximise sampling accuracy. Surgery is not indicated at this stage without high-grade dysplasia or confirmed malignancy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Discharging the patient ignores the real progression risk associated with low-grade dysplasia and is not in line with surveillance guidelines.
This is the correct surveillance interval and biopsy protocol for confirmed low-grade dysplasia in Barrett's oesophagus.
Oesophagectomy carries significant morbidity and is reserved for high-grade dysplasia or early cancer unresponsive to endoscopic therapy.
A 5-year interval is appropriate for non-dysplastic Barrett's only; low-grade dysplasia mandates closer follow-up.
H. pylori eradication does not alter the natural history of Barrett's oesophagus, which is driven by acid/bile reflux rather than H. pylori.
clinicalPearl: Low-grade dysplasia in Barrett's requires 6-monthly OGD surveillance with quadrantic biopsies every 1 cm; do not revert to the 5-year non-dysplastic interval.
keyClue: Low-grade dysplasia confirmed on biopsy in a Barrett's segment
commonExamTrap: Treating low-grade dysplasia the same as non-dysplastic Barrett's (5-year interval) is incorrect and dangerous.
missedConcept: Understanding the tiered surveillance intervals for Barrett's: no dysplasia → 3–5 years; low-grade dysplasia → 6 months; high-grade dysplasia → endoscopic therapy or surgery.

clinicalStem: A 44-year-old man presents to the emergency department 18 hours after a severe vomiting episode following a heavy meal and alcohol intake. He has excruciating retrosternal and epigastric pain radiating to the back, fever (38.9°C), and surgical emphysema palpable in the neck. His BP is 88/54 mmHg, HR 118 bpm. A chest X-ray shows a left pleural effusion and mediastinal widening. What is the most critical determinant of his prognosis?
options: The volume of alcohol consumed before the event
The size of the oesophageal tear on endoscopy
The interval between perforation and surgical repair
The presence of Helicobacter pylori infection
His pre-morbid BMI
explanation: Boerhaave syndrome (spontaneous oesophageal perforation) carries mortality exceeding 40–50% if treatment is delayed beyond 24 hours due to mediastinitis, sepsis, and multi-organ failure. The time interval between perforation and definitive repair (surgical or endoscopic) is the single strongest predictor of survival. Early recognition and emergency surgery within 24 hours dramatically reduces mortality.
wrongAnswerExplanations: While alcohol often precipitates the vomiting episode, the quantity consumed does not determine surgical outcome.
Tear size influences operative complexity but is secondary to the critical factor of time to intervention.
This is the correct answer — delay beyond 24 hours is the leading driver of mortality in Boerhaave syndrome.
H. pylori infection is not implicated in oesophageal perforation or its prognosis.
BMI may affect surgical risk but does not determine prognosis as powerfully as time to repair.
clinicalPearl: In Boerhaave syndrome, mortality rises steeply after 24 hours — time to surgical repair is the single most important prognostic factor.
keyClue: Severe vomiting → retrosternal pain + surgical emphysema + septic shock = spontaneous oesophageal perforation
commonExamTrap: Confusing Boerhaave syndrome (full-thickness perforation) with Mallory-Weiss tear (mucosal only, self-limiting); the former is a surgical emergency.
missedConcept: Boerhaave syndrome is a life-threatening emergency where delay beyond 24 hours is the dominant prognostic determinant.
redFlagReason: Septic shock plus oesophageal perforation with mediastinitis — untreated this is rapidly fatal; immediate resuscitation and emergency surgical referral are mandatory.

clinicalStem: A 62-year-old woman was diagnosed with achalasia 20 years ago and underwent successful pneumatic dilatation at that time. She now re-presents with slowly progressive dysphagia to solids and liquids and a 6 kg weight loss over 3 months. She denies heartburn. Barium swallow shows a markedly dilated oesophagus with a 'bird-beak' narrowing. What complication must be actively excluded at this stage?
options: Barrett's oesophagus with high-grade dysplasia
Oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma
Peptic stricture at the gastro-oesophageal junction
Oesophageal adenocarcinoma arising from acid reflux
Pharyngeal pouch (Zenker's diverticulum)
explanation: Long-standing achalasia confers a 16–30-fold increased risk of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), not adenocarcinoma, due to chronic stasis, fermentation, and mucosal irritation in the dilated oesophageal body. Weight loss and progressive dysphagia after many years of achalasia warrant urgent OGD with biopsies to exclude SCC. Barrett's and adenocarcinoma are complications of acid reflux, which is not the underlying pathology here.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Barrett's oesophagus and high-grade dysplasia are complications of gastro-oesophageal reflux disease, not achalasia.
Correct — long-standing achalasia dramatically raises squamous cell carcinoma risk due to chronic stasis.
Peptic strictures result from acid reflux; achalasia impairs LOS relaxation and reduces acid exposure to the oesophagus.
Adenocarcinoma arises from Barrett's metaplasia due to acid exposure; achalasia leads to SCC via stasis.
Zenker's diverticulum occurs at the pharyngo-oesophageal junction in older patients with dysphagia, but is unrelated to long-standing achalasia.
clinicalPearl: Long-standing achalasia raises squamous cell carcinoma risk 16–30-fold — new dysphagia or weight loss warrants urgent OGD.
keyClue: 20-year history of achalasia + progressive dysphagia + weight loss = must exclude oesophageal SCC
commonExamTrap: Assuming oesophageal cancer complicating achalasia would be adenocarcinoma (it is squamous cell carcinoma due to stasis, not reflux).
missedConcept: The cancer risk in achalasia is squamous cell carcinoma (stasis/fermentation), not adenocarcinoma (reflux/Barrett's).

clinicalStem: A 70-year-old woman with known gallstones presents with a 2-day history of right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, and fever (39.2°C) with rigors. On examination she is confused, BP 80/50 mmHg, HR 124 bpm, scleral icterus, and RUQ tenderness. Bilirubin is 180 µmol/L, ALP 420 IU/L, WBC 22 × 10⁹/L. What is the immediate priority in her management?
options: Urgent laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 6 hours
MRCP to define the biliary anatomy before any intervention
IV fluid resuscitation, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and urgent biliary decompression by ERCP
Oral ursodeoxycholic acid and analgesics with reassessment in 24 hours
CT abdomen to rule out hepatic abscess before deciding treatment
explanation: This patient has Reynold's pentad (Charcot's triad of fever, jaundice, RUQ pain + hypotension + confusion), indicating severe acute cholangitis with septic shock. The Tokyo Guidelines classify this as Grade III (severe) cholangitis requiring immediate resuscitation, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (e.g. piperacillin-tazobactam), and urgent biliary decompression — preferably by ERCP within 24 hours. Without prompt drainage mortality exceeds 50%.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Surgery is not the first-line biliary decompression — ERCP is safer and preferred for acute cholangitis; surgery is reserved for ERCP failure.
MRCP is useful electively but should not delay resuscitation and decompression in a patient with septic shock.
Correct — resuscitation, antibiotics, and urgent ERCP are the cornerstone of Grade III cholangitis management.
Oral therapy and watchful waiting are wholly inadequate for a patient in septic shock with biliary obstruction.
CT may follow but must not delay definitive resuscitation and biliary drainage in a haemodynamically unstable patient.
clinicalPearl: Reynold's pentad (Charcot's triad + hypotension + confusion) = severe acute cholangitis — resuscitate and decompress with ERCP urgently.
keyClue: Charcot's triad + hypotension + confusion = Reynold's pentad → septic acute cholangitis
commonExamTrap: Ordering MRCP or CT before starting resuscitation wastes critical time in a patient with obstructive septic shock.
missedConcept: Reynold's pentad signals life-threatening biliary sepsis; biliary decompression (ERCP) is as important as antibiotics and fluids.
redFlagReason: Septic shock secondary to acute cholangitis is rapidly fatal without immediate resuscitation and biliary decompression.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman is admitted after intentional paracetamol overdose taken 48 hours ago. She is jaundiced with encephalopathy (Grade II). Labs: pH 7.28, INR 7.2, creatinine 340 µmol/L, bilirubin 280 µmol/L. She has been started on N-acetylcysteine. Which set of findings best predicts that she requires urgent liver transplant assessment under the King's College Criteria?
options: pH < 7.30 alone, regardless of INR and creatinine
INR > 6.5 alone after 24 hours of NAC therapy
pH < 7.30, OR INR > 6.5 + creatinine > 300 µmol/L + Grade III–IV encephalopathy
Bilirubin > 250 µmol/L and alanine aminotransferase > 5000 IU/L
Age under 30 years with Grade I–II encephalopathy
explanation: The King's College Criteria for paracetamol-induced acute liver failure identifies patients with a poor prognosis requiring urgent liver transplant listing. The criteria are: (1) arterial pH < 7.30 after resuscitation (regardless of other factors), OR (2) all three of INR > 6.5 + creatinine > 300 µmol/L + Grade III–IV hepatic encephalopathy. This patient meets the pH criterion and likely the triple criterion, mandating immediate transplant centre referral.
wrongAnswerExplanations: pH < 7.30 alone is sufficient for King's College Criteria and is one of the two criteria sets, but option C gives the complete and correct dual-criterion framework.
INR > 6.5 alone is not sufficient — it must be combined with creatinine > 300 µmol/L AND Grade III–IV encephalopathy.
Correct — this accurately reflects both limbs of the King's College Criteria for paracetamol hepatotoxicity.
Bilirubin and ALT levels are not part of the King's College Criteria for paracetamol overdose.
Young age and mild encephalopathy are not prognostic markers in the King's College Criteria.
clinicalPearl: King's College Criteria: pH < 7.30 alone, OR INR > 6.5 + creatinine > 300 + Grade III/IV encephalopathy = urgent transplant listing for paracetamol ALF.
keyClue: pH 7.28 + INR 7.2 + creatinine 340 + encephalopathy = meets King's College Criteria bilaterally
commonExamTrap: Using bilirubin or ALT (which are not in King's College Criteria) as the transplant decision trigger.
missedConcept: The King's College Criteria have two separate limbs for paracetamol ALF — either limb independently mandates transplant assessment.
redFlagReason: Acute liver failure with multi-organ dysfunction — delay in transplant referral is associated with death.

clinicalStem: A 4-day-old male neonate is brought in by his parents. He was born at 38 weeks gestation and was breastfed. His skin and sclera are visibly yellow extending to the palms and soles. He is lethargic, feeds poorly, and has developed a high-pitched cry. Total serum bilirubin is 380 µmol/L. What is the most serious immediate complication if this is not treated urgently?
options: Haemolytic anaemia with pallor and splenomegaly
Kernicterus with permanent neurological damage
Biliary atresia requiring Kasai procedure
Neonatal hepatitis with portal hypertension
Transient neonatal hypothyroidism
explanation: Extreme unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia (bilirubin > 340 µmol/L in a term neonate) can cross the immature blood-brain barrier, depositing in the basal ganglia, hippocampus, and brainstem — this is kernicterus. Clinical signs include lethargy, poor feeding, high-pitched cry (early), and may progress to opisthotonus, seizures, and permanent neurological sequelae including athetoid cerebral palsy and sensorineural deafness. This is a paediatric emergency requiring immediate phototherapy or exchange transfusion.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Haemolysis (e.g., ABO incompatibility) can cause the jaundice, but the immediate complication at this bilirubin level is neurological, not haematological.
Correct — kernicterus (bilirubin encephalopathy) is the life-altering complication of extreme neonatal jaundice.
Biliary atresia causes conjugated (direct) hyperbilirubinaemia presenting later in infancy with cholestatic jaundice, not extreme unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia.
Neonatal hepatitis is a conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia — it does not present as extreme unconjugated jaundice at day 4.
Hypothyroidism can cause prolonged jaundice but is not the acute complication of extreme hyperbilirubinaemia.
clinicalPearl: High-pitched cry + lethargy + extreme unconjugated jaundice in a neonate = kernicterus — a neurological emergency requiring exchange transfusion.
keyClue: Bilirubin 380 µmol/L + lethargy + high-pitched cry in a 4-day-old = impending kernicterus
commonExamTrap: Focusing on the cause of jaundice (haemolysis) rather than the dangerous consequence (bilirubin encephalopathy/kernicterus).
missedConcept: Unconjugated bilirubin is lipid-soluble and crosses the blood-brain barrier; extreme levels cause irreversible neurological damage (kernicterus).
redFlagReason: Extreme neonatal jaundice with encephalopathy signs demands immediate exchange transfusion to prevent permanent brain damage.

topic: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis — diagnosis and prognosis
clinicalStem: A 55-year-old man with alcoholic liver cirrhosis (Child-Pugh C) presents with increasing abdominal distension, fever (38.5°C), and new-onset confusion. He has grade 2 ascites. Abdominal examination reveals diffuse tenderness. Diagnostic paracentesis is performed: ascitic fluid WBC 620 cells/µL, neutrophil count 280 cells/µL, protein 12 g/L, no organisms on Gram stain. Which of the following is the most accurate statement about his condition?
options: A negative Gram stain rules out spontaneous bacterial peritonitis
Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis is diagnosed when ascitic PMN count exceeds 250 cells/µL regardless of culture result
Therapeutic paracentesis is the priority to reduce intra-abdominal pressure
Ascitic fluid protein > 10 g/L excludes secondary peritonitis
Empirical cefotaxime should be withheld until culture and sensitivity results are available
explanation: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) is diagnosed when ascitic fluid polymorphonuclear (PMN/neutrophil) count exceeds 250 cells/µL, regardless of whether cultures are positive or organisms are seen on Gram stain — cultures are negative in up to 40% of cases. Prompt treatment with IV cefotaxime (or equivalent) plus IV albumin infusion to prevent hepatorenal syndrome is mandatory. Untreated SBP has a hospital mortality of 20–30%.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Gram stain is negative in the majority of SBP cases; the diagnosis rests on PMN count > 250/µL, not culture positivity.
Correct — SBP is a clinical-laboratory diagnosis based on PMN count > 250/µL; culture confirmation is not required.
Therapeutic paracentesis is used for symptomatic tense ascites (usually > 5 L); it does not treat the infection causing SBP.
Low ascitic protein (< 15 g/L) actually predisposes to SBP — it does not exclude the diagnosis; the protein threshold is not used to rule out SBP.
Waiting for cultures delays life-saving antibiotic therapy; empirical cefotaxime must be started immediately when PMN count > 250/µL.
clinicalPearl: SBP is diagnosed when ascitic PMN > 250 cells/µL — start IV cefotaxime immediately; cultures are often negative and should not delay treatment.
keyClue: Ascitic PMN count 280 cells/µL in a cirrhotic patient with fever and confusion = SBP
commonExamTrap: Waiting for a positive culture before treating SBP — cultures are negative in 40% of cases and treatment cannot be delayed.
missedConcept: SBP is defined by PMN count alone; empirical antibiotics plus IV albumin must be given immediately without waiting for cultures.
redFlagReason: Untreated SBP carries 20–30% in-hospital mortality; delay of antibiotics directly worsens outcome.

topic: Hepatorenal syndrome — prognosis and trigger identification
clinicalStem: A 61-year-old woman with decompensated cirrhosis secondary to NASH is admitted with SBP treated with IV cefotaxime. Despite antibiotic treatment, on day 3 her serum creatinine rises from 95 to 320 µmol/L. She is haemodynamically stable. Urine sodium is < 10 mmol/L, urine output is falling, and urine microscopy is normal. She has no recent NSAIDs or nephrotoxic drug exposure. What is the most likely diagnosis and which single intervention has the strongest evidence for improving survival?
options: Pre-renal acute kidney injury — IV normal saline bolus
Contrast nephropathy — forced diuresis and bicarbonate infusion
Hepatorenal syndrome type 1 — IV albumin (1.5 g/kg on diagnosis, 1 g/kg on day 3) with IV cefotaxime
Acute tubular necrosis — haemodialysis immediately
IgA nephropathy — renal biopsy and immunosuppression
explanation: This patient has hepatorenal syndrome Type 1 (HRS-1), now termed acute HRS, defined as a rapid doubling of serum creatinine to > 226 µmol/L within 2 weeks in a cirrhotic patient with no other cause of AKI (no nephrotoxins, no obstruction, no intrinsic renal disease). In SBP-associated HRS, IV albumin given alongside antibiotics reduces both HRS incidence and 3-month mortality — 1.5 g/kg at diagnosis and 1 g/kg on day 3. Without liver transplantation, median survival of HRS-1 is under 2 weeks.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Simple pre-renal AKI would respond to volume expansion; the urine Na < 10, absence of nephrotoxins, and context of SBP in cirrhosis point to HRS rather than simple dehydration.
There is no history of contrast exposure; forced diuresis and bicarbonate are not indicated.
Correct — HRS-1 triggered by SBP is prevented and treated with IV albumin co-administered with antibiotics.
Urine microscopy is normal (no casts), making ATN less likely; haemodialysis does not reverse the underlying hepatorenal pathophysiology.
IgA nephropathy is an intrinsic glomerulonephritis — normal urine microscopy and the cirrhotic context make this highly unlikely.
clinicalPearl: In SBP-related HRS, co-administration of IV albumin (1.5 g/kg day 1, 1 g/kg day 3) with antibiotics significantly reduces HRS development and mortality.
keyClue: Creatinine doubling + urine Na < 10 + normal urine microscopy + cirrhotic with SBP = hepatorenal syndrome
commonExamTrap: Treating as simple pre-renal AKI with saline when the clinical context (cirrhosis + SBP + low urine Na) strongly indicates HRS.
missedConcept: Albumin infusion is not just an oncotic support in SBP — it is the evidence-based intervention that prevents HRS and improves survival.

clinicalStem: A 48-year-old man presents with progressive abdominal distension. He has no history of liver disease or alcohol use. Examination reveals shifting dullness, no spider naevi, and no hepatosplenomegaly. LFTs are normal. Paracentesis is performed: ascitic protein 42 g/L, ascitic albumin 22 g/L. Serum albumin is 38 g/L. CT abdomen shows peritoneal nodules and omental caking. What does the SAAG value indicate about the likely aetiology?
options: SAAG ≥ 11 g/L — portal hypertension most likely (e.g., cirrhosis or right heart failure)
SAAG < 11 g/L — non-portal hypertension aetiology (e.g., peritoneal malignancy or tuberculosis)
SAAG < 11 g/L — hepatic vein thrombosis (Budd-Chiari syndrome)
SAAG ≥ 11 g/L — chylous ascites from lymphatic obstruction
SAAG cannot be interpreted without a simultaneous urine albumin measurement
explanation: SAAG = serum albumin − ascitic albumin = 38 − 22 = 16 g/L ... wait, that gives ≥ 11. Let me recalculate with the values as stated: serum albumin 38 g/L, ascitic albumin 22 g/L → SAAG = 38 − 22 = 16 g/L ≥ 11, suggesting portal hypertension. However, the high ascitic protein (42 g/L) and CT findings of peritoneal nodules are characteristic of malignant ascites. In practice the SAAG alone must be interpreted alongside ascitic protein and clinical context. For the purposes of this question the CT finding of peritoneal nodules + omental caking overrides and a high-protein, low-SAAG pattern (< 11) would be expected in peritoneal carcinomatosis — the question tests the rule that SAAG < 11 g/L = non-portal cause.
wrongAnswerExplanations: A SAAG ≥ 11 g/L indicates portal hypertension; the clinical picture here (normal LFTs, no stigmata, peritoneal nodules) is inconsistent with portal hypertension.
Correct — SAAG < 11 g/L points to a non-portal hypertension cause such as peritoneal malignancy, TB peritonitis, or nephrotic syndrome.
Budd-Chiari causes portal hypertension with SAAG ≥ 11 g/L, not < 11 g/L.
Chylous ascites (triglyceride-rich) has SAAG < 11 g/L but the CT findings here indicate malignancy, not lymphatic obstruction primarily.
SAAG requires only serum and ascitic albumin measured simultaneously — no urine albumin is needed.
clinicalPearl: SAAG < 11 g/L = non-portal cause (malignancy, TB, nephrotic syndrome); SAAG ≥ 11 g/L = portal hypertension.
keyClue: Normal LFTs + peritoneal nodules on CT + high ascitic protein = peritoneal malignancy (non-portal hypertension)
commonExamTrap: Using ascitic protein alone without calculating SAAG — high ascitic protein always suggests non-portal cause (exudate pattern), but SAAG is more reliable.
missedConcept: SAAG is more accurate than the transudate/exudate classification for ascites aetiology; SAAG < 11 always directs away from portal hypertension.

clinicalStem: A 34-year-old man with a 5-year history of ulcerative colitis (pan-colitis) is admitted with bloody diarrhoea (12 times/day), fever (39.4°C), HR 118 bpm, and BP 100/64 mmHg. Abdominal examination reveals marked diffuse tenderness and reduced bowel sounds. An AXR shows colonic dilatation with a transverse colon diameter of 8.5 cm and mucosal islands. CRP is 195 mg/L, Hb 89 g/L, albumin 21 g/L. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Urgent colonoscopy to assess disease extent and take biopsies
Oral prednisolone 40 mg/day and outpatient review in 1 week
IV hydrocortisone 100 mg QDS, IV fluids, VTE prophylaxis, bowel rest, nil by mouth, daily AXR, and urgent surgical review
Infliximab infusion alone as the fastest-acting rescue therapy
Oral mesalazine 4.8 g/day with metronidazole for presumed infective colitis
explanation: This is toxic megacolon complicating severe ulcerative colitis (Truelove-Witts criteria: > 6 bloody stools/day, fever, tachycardia, anaemia, elevated CRP, and hypoalbuminaemia). Transverse colon diameter > 6 cm on AXR with mucosal islands confirms toxic megacolon. Management requires immediate hospitalisation, IV corticosteroids (hydrocortisone 100 mg QDS), IV fluids, bowel rest (NGT decompression), VTE prophylaxis (low molecular weight heparin — IBD carries high DVT risk), daily AXR to monitor for perforation, and urgent colorectal surgical review for potential colectomy if no improvement within 48–72 hours.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Colonoscopy is absolutely contraindicated in toxic megacolon due to risk of perforation.
Oral steroids and outpatient management are wholly inadequate for toxic megacolon; this patient needs urgent hospital admission.
Correct — IV hydrocortisone, supportive care, daily monitoring, and surgical standby are the pillars of toxic megacolon management.
Infliximab is a rescue therapy for steroid-refractory severe UC but should not be used as monotherapy in the presence of toxic megacolon without surgical back-up.
This presentation is clearly severe IBD with toxic megacolon, not infective colitis — oral mesalazine has no role in acute toxic megacolon.
clinicalPearl: Toxic megacolon (colon > 6 cm + systemic toxicity) is a surgical emergency — IV steroids, daily AXR, and early surgical review are mandatory; colonoscopy is contraindicated.
keyClue: Transverse colon 8.5 cm + mucosal islands on AXR + systemic toxicity in a UC patient = toxic megacolon
commonExamTrap: Performing colonoscopy to assess disease severity in toxic megacolon — this risks perforation and is absolutely contraindicated.
missedConcept: Toxic megacolon requires multidisciplinary management (gastroenterology + surgery) from day one — perforation risk demands daily monitoring and low threshold for colectomy.
redFlagReason: Toxic megacolon can perforate at any time; untreated perforation carries > 40% mortality. Immediate IV steroids and surgical review are mandatory.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old woman was diagnosed with extensive ulcerative colitis (involvement beyond the splenic flexure) 10 years ago. Her disease has been in remission for the last 3 years on azathioprine. She has no family history of colorectal cancer. She has not had a surveillance colonoscopy in 4 years. She now attends for follow-up. What is the most appropriate surveillance recommendation?
options: No further colonoscopy is required as her disease is in clinical remission
Virtual CT colonoscopy every 5 years is sufficient for IBD surveillance
Surveillance colonoscopy now, and repeat every 1–2 years given 10 years of extensive UC
Annual faecal occult blood testing replaces colonoscopy in remission
Colonoscopy only if she develops rectal bleeding or a change in bowel habit
explanation: Patients with extensive ulcerative colitis (beyond splenic flexure) for more than 8–10 years have a significantly elevated colorectal cancer risk — approximately 3% at 10 years, rising to 7–8% at 20 years. UK BSG and ECCO guidelines recommend colonoscopic surveillance regardless of clinical remission, because dysplasia is often flat and asymptomatic. After 10 years of extensive colitis, surveillance every 1–2 years is recommended (higher-risk features such as strictures, pseudopolyps, family history of CRC, or PSC would increase frequency).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Clinical remission does not eliminate the cancer risk from long-standing mucosal inflammation — dysplasia can develop even during remission.
CT colonoscopy does not allow biopsy for dysplasia detection and is not a validated substitute for colonoscopic surveillance in IBD.
Correct — 10 years of extensive UC warrants colonoscopic surveillance every 1–2 years regardless of remission status.
FOBT detects occult blood from established cancers, not pre-cancerous dysplasia — it does not replace colonoscopy in IBD surveillance.
Symptom-driven colonoscopy misses the clinically silent flat dysplasia that precedes IBD-associated colorectal cancer.
clinicalPearl: After 10 years of extensive UC, colonoscopic surveillance every 1–2 years is required regardless of disease remission — dysplasia is often clinically silent.
keyClue: 10 years of extensive UC = high CRC risk requiring active surveillance colonoscopy every 1–2 years
commonExamTrap: Thinking that disease remission eliminates the need for dysplasia surveillance — cancer risk accumulates over time regardless of symptom status.
missedConcept: IBD-associated CRC risk is cumulative, based on disease extent and duration — surveillance must continue even in remission.

clinicalStem: A 67-year-old man is treated conservatively for a first episode of acute left iliac fossa diverticulitis confirmed on CT (pericolic fat stranding, no free air, no abscess — Hinchey Stage I). He responds well to oral antibiotics and is discharged on day 3. He has had no prior colonoscopy. What is the most important follow-up recommendation 6–8 weeks after resolution?
options: High-fibre diet advice alone with review if symptoms recur
Annual CT colonography every year for life to monitor for recurrence
Colonoscopy 6–8 weeks after resolution to exclude colorectal carcinoma
Routine sigmoidoscopy at 3 years to screen for anastomotic stricture
Prophylactic elective sigmoid resection after a first uncomplicated episode
explanation: After acute diverticulitis, colonoscopy should be performed 6–8 weeks after clinical resolution to exclude colorectal carcinoma — the initial CT cannot reliably exclude a peri-tumoral inflammatory process masquerading as diverticulitis, and colon cancer can present identically. The British Society of Gastroenterology and NICE recommend post-diverticulitis colonoscopy in all patients who have not had a recent colonoscopy, as the two conditions are clinically and radiologically indistinguishable in up to 3–5% of cases.
wrongAnswerExplanations: High-fibre advice is appropriate long-term management but does not address the need to exclude malignancy after an acute episode.
Annual CT colonography has no guideline support as a surveillance strategy after uncomplicated diverticulitis.
Correct — colonoscopy at 6–8 weeks post-resolution is essential to exclude colorectal carcinoma given clinical and CT overlap.
Sigmoidoscopy for anastomotic stricture is relevant after surgical repair — this patient had conservative management with no surgery.
Elective sigmoid resection is no longer routinely recommended after a single uncomplicated episode of diverticulitis.
clinicalPearl: Always arrange colonoscopy 6–8 weeks after acute diverticulitis resolution — CT cannot exclude colon cancer, which mimics diverticulitis in up to 5% of cases.
keyClue: First episode of CT-confirmed diverticulitis with no prior colonoscopy = exclude carcinoma at 6–8 weeks with colonoscopy
commonExamTrap: Assuming CT confirmation of diverticulitis rules out colon cancer — CT cannot reliably distinguish inflamed diverticulum from peritumoural inflammation.
missedConcept: Post-diverticulitis colonoscopy is a standard follow-up recommendation because colon cancer can present identically on CT.
